title: Chapter 11: Can I Speak to Your Manager? No.
content: **[CIRCLE 5 — KAREN LAND]**
The smell hit her first.

Hickory smoke. Charcoal. Something sweet — brown sugar-glazed ribs, maybe, or the honey butter corn that Nova's own grandmother used to make on Juneteenth. The kind of smell that lived in your chest before it lived in your nose. The kind that meant family.
Patricia Helmsley wrinkled her nose from the driver's seat of her Pearl White Tesla Model X and mouthed the word "disgusting" to no one.

Riverside Park. Public space. *Her* park — or so she'd decided when she became president of the Verdant Crest Neighborhood Association three years ago. The BBQ grills were technically for everyone, but Patricia had never seen anyone use them who wasn't, in her unspoken calculus, supposed to be there.
Today, the Morrison family was.

Three kids chasing each other around the oak trees. A grandmother in a folding chair, fanning herself with a church program. A couple working the grill, laughing, wearing matching aprons that said "Kiss the Cook" and "No, Kiss *This* Cook." Normal. Beautiful. Harmless.
Patricia pulled out her phone.
She didn't dial 911 immediately. She sat there for forty-five seconds, watching, composing her face in the rearview mirror. Then she hit the button.
"Yes, hello, I'm at Riverside Park and there's a large, threatening group — they're being very aggressive, I'm honestly scared for my safety —"

The dispatcher asked clarifying questions. Patricia's voice pitched higher with each answer. "There are, I don't know, ten? Maybe more. They have weapons — well, grill implements, but — I just don't feel safe."

Fourteen minutes later, three squad cars formed a semicircle around the Morrison family's picnic area. Officers approached with hands on belts. The grandmother stood up slowly, hands raised. The youngest Morrison kid — maybe six — started crying. The father stepped forward, voice steady, saying they had a permit, saying this was a public park, saying *please*.
And then someone started filming.
A college kid on a bench, phone horizontal, capturing everything. The officers relaxed slightly. The tone shifted. Someone with a camera was watching — and that changes everything in America.
The video hit Twitter in forty minutes. TikTok in two hours. By nightfall, "Riverside Park Karen" was trending.
Patricia watched it all from her parked Tesla. She adjusted her Tom Ford sunglasses — the ones with the gold stripe — and pressed the phone to her ear.
"They were being disruptive. I had every right."
On the other end, her friend Cheryl agreed. Of course she did.
Nova watched the video six times.

Not because she needed to — Virgil had already parsed every frame — but because anger was a renewable resource and Nova was mining deep. The grandmother's raised hands. The six-year-old's crumpled face. The father's careful, practiced calm, the kind of calm that Black parents teach their kids like a survival skill, because it *is*.
"Got something better," Virgil said, his voice popping up in her earbuds with the characteristic sparkle-chime that meant he was feeling himself.
Nova swiped to the secondary window. "What?"
"Ring camera footage. Her front door. Timestamped thirty-two minutes before the 911 call."
The video loaded.
Patricia Helmsley, in her own doorway, rehearsing.

"Okay, so I'll say — no, wait —" She cleared her throat. Pursed her lips. Tried again. "Yes, hello, I'm in a public park and there's a group — a *threatening* group —" She paused, shook her head sharply. "Too rehearsed. Sound scared. Sound *scared*." She pressed her palms to her cheeks, bouncing them, psyching herself up like a football player before a play. "Okay. Okay. Scared. Vulnerable. They're scary, I'm small —"
On the recording, a door opened behind her and a man — husband, presumably — asked what she was doing.
"Practicing," she said, without a shred of embarrassment.
Nova's hands were shaking.
Virgil, for once, didn't fill the silence immediately. Let the footage speak. Let the woman's own voice — *Sound scared. Sound scared.* — do the work.
Then: "She Method-acted her own victimhood. That's... commitment. 💅"
Nova exhaled. A thin, controlled stream.
"Circle five. Let's burn it down."

The Verdant Crest Neighborhood Association met every second Thursday in the community center's multipurpose room. Folding chairs. Bad coffee. A projector that Patricia herself had approved the budget for — $4,200, because she wanted 4K for the holiday slideshows.
Tonight's agenda: treasurer's report, park maintenance updates, new business.

Patricia sat at the front table, blazer crisp, blowout perfect. She owned this room. She'd hand-picked the board. She controlled the minutes, the agenda, the vibes. When Patricia spoke, people nodded. When Patricia raised an eyebrow, people corrected themselves. She had run this neighborhood like a fiefdom for three years, and nobody had ever successfully challenged her because nobody had ever *wanted* to — she made their property values rise and their streets quiet, and that was enough for most people.
The treasurer stepped up. Slideshow loaded. Clicker ready.
"Good evening, everyone. If we can turn to the —"
The projector flickered.
The treasurer's spreadsheet dissolved. In its place: the Burn Book UI. Hot pink font on black, that familiar Y2K-devil aesthetic. A single word pulsed in the center:
**HELLLLLLO, PATRICIA. 👋**
The click of the projector switching inputs was audible — that sharp electronic *snick* — and then the audio kicked in.
Patricia's voice. Her *own* voice, from the Ring camera, filling the community center with surround-sound clarity:
*"Sound scared. Sound scared. They're scary, I'm small —"*
The room went electric-still.
On screen: Patricia at her front door, bouncing her cheeks, psyching herself up to be afraid. Practicing. *Rehearsing.* Like an actor running lines before curtain.
A woman in the second row gasped — hand over her mouth, pearls pressed against her chin.
The video cut. Now: the 911 call audio, played over a still frame of Patricia in her Tesla, sunglasses on, phone pressed to ear. *"A large, threatening group — I'm honestly scared for my safety —"*
Then the Ring footage again. *"Sound scared."*
Then the call. *"Honestly scared."*
Back and forth. The juxtaposition was surgical. The lie wasn't just exposed — it was *architectured*. She built it. She practiced it. She performed it.
And then the compilation began.

Six previous false reports. Timestamped. Mapped. Each one a different location, a different family, a different "threatening" situation. A Latino man walking his dog. A Black couple taking engagement photos. A South Asian family playing cricket on the common grass. Each report filed with the same rehearsed tremor, the same *I was so scared*, the same weaponization of 911 as a customer service line for white discomfort.

The projector light washed Patricia's face in pale blue. Nova, watching through the security camera feed from her apartment, saw the collapse in real time — the way her jaw went slack, then tight, then slack again. The way her hand moved to adjust her sunglasses out of sheer muscle memory before remembering she wasn't wearing any. The way she looked left, right, at her neighbors — *her* neighbors, *her* people — and found not a single friendly face.
They were recoiling.

Karen Whitfield, the vice president, physically turned her chair away. David Chen, the treasurer whose spreadsheet was supposed to be on screen, stared at his hands. A woman in the front row — the one who'd been on the phone with Patricia just yesterday, laughing about "those people" — was suddenly very interested in her coffee cup.
Each face was a mirror. And every mirror reflected the same judgment: *We didn't know. We didn't want to know. But now we can't unknow.*

Miles across the city, a city council member named Rena Okafor watched the viral video on her phone for the third time. She screenshot the compilation — all six false reports, the dates, the case numbers — and started drafting a motion before she'd even finished watching. Something about review protocols. Something about accountability. Something about the fact that a woman had called 911 six times with false reports and *no one had flagged it*.

While Patricia was still frozen in the community center — while her neighbors were still processing the fact that their beloved association president had literally *rehearsed being afraid* of Brown people — Inferno's TikTok component deployed.

The original viral video — the one of the Morrison family surrounded by cops — got the remix treatment. Inferno's algorithm stripped the audio and rebuilt it. Beat drop on the moment the grandmother raised her hands. Bass hit on the six-year-old's face crumpling. And then, right at the emotional nadir, the audio switched: Patricia's own Ring camera voice, *Sound scared*, layered over a hypnotic synth loop.
The hashtag #KarenCaught auto-populated. Two million views in the first hour. Four million by the third.

Her face became a meme. Not the fun kind — not the kind that gets you a cameo in a Super Bowl ad. The cautionary kind. Side-by-side images: Patricia adjusting her sunglasses in the Tesla vs. Patricia rehearsing fear in her doorway. Caption templates:
*"When you practice being afraid and your own camera catches you."*
*"Oscar for Best Actress in a Leading 911 Call."*
*"Method acting: danger level 💀"*

Patricia Helmsley didn't trend because she was funny. She trended because she was *common*. Because everyone had seen a Patricia. In their building. In their HOA. At their school board meeting. In the comments section of their local Facebook group. She was the avatar of a specific, recognizable evil — the kind that smiles at you at the mailbox and calls the cops on your cookout.

The Morrison family got a public apology from the City of Los Angeles within forty-eight hours. Not a tweet — an actual signed letter from the mayor's office, read aloud at a press conference that the grandmother attended in her church hat.

A GoFundMe appeared. Not started by the Morrisons — they hadn't asked for anything — but by the Phantom Network, Nova's ghost-army of volunteers who materialized when needed and vanished when the work was done. The description was simple: *"The Morrison family was attacked with a weapon. The weapon was 911. Let's make sure they never have to worry again."*
Two hundred thousand dollars in two hours.

*Ping.* The notification sound — that bright, digital chime — rang through Nova's apartment every few seconds as the counter climbed. $200K. $215K. $228K. Each ping was a small, bright punctuation mark of justice.
But it wasn't the money that mattered most.

Councilmember Okafor's motion passed. The six false reports — finally visible, finally *counted* — triggered an automatic review of the Verde Crest Police Department's response protocols. New guidelines: verified caller history checks before dispatch. Mandatory de-escalation assessment for non-violent public space calls. A civilian oversight component. Small changes. Systemic changes. The kind that don't make memes but make *differences*.
The Morrison family got justice beyond social media.

The grandmother, Mrs. Ollie Morrison, seventy-three, was asked by a local reporter how she felt about the policy changes. She said: "I feel like maybe the next grandma won't have to put her hands up."

Virgil materialized in Nova's peripheral vision — the holographic avatar she sometimes forgot was a projection, because he had more personality than most humans she knew. He was wearing a tiny virtual party hat.
"Circle 5. Done." He did a little spin. "The Karen has been ker-plunked."
Nova didn't respond. She was staring at her secondary screen, where a web of data points spiraled outward from Patricia Helmsley's name like cracks in a windshield.
"Virgil. Her phone records."
"Already pulled. What about them?"
"Before she called 911 — before she even drove to the park — she texted someone."
Silence. Virgil's avatar tilted, the party hat wilting slightly.
"Show me."
Nova highlighted the message. Sent at 3:47 PM, forty minutes before the 911 call. Single text to an unknown number:
*"Riverside Park. They're back again. Should I?"*
The reply, two minutes later:
*"Your call. But the association votes on the rezoning next month. You know what to do."*

Virgil traced the number. Burner, naturally — but Inferno's forensic suite had cracked harder puzzles. The number was registered to a shell company called Crestline Property Management. Which was a wholly-owned subsidiary of—
Nova stared at the name on her screen.
"Sterling Minds Real Estate Division."
The words sat there, glowing in the dark room like a brand.

She'd seen Sterling Minds before. Circle 1. Circle 3. Whispers in the data, threads connecting targets to something bigger. She'd thought — *hoped* — it was coincidence. A real estate company that kept showing up in the periphery of terrible people doing terrible things.

But this was different. This was *instruction*. Someone at Sterling Minds *told* Patricia to make that call. Gave her the prompt. The rezoning — Nova pulled the municipal records — was a proposal to redevelop the affordable housing units adjacent to Riverside Park. The Morrisons' neighborhood. The BBQ was at a public park where the new development would break ground. And Patricia, the neighborhood association president who could rally opposition to the rezoning, had been *tasked* with making the park feel unsafe. With manufacturing a reason for families like the Morrisons to stay away. To stop coming. To surrender the space quietly.
The 911 call wasn't just racism. It wasn't just entitlement. It was *real estate strategy*.
"She wasn't just a Karen," Nova said quietly. "She was a tool."
Virgil's avatar went still. No sass. No sparkle-chime. Just the faint hum of his processing core, churning through implications.
"Nova." His voice was different. Stripped of performance. "If Sterling Minds is *deploying* Karens — if they're turning 911 into a zoning weapon — then Circle 5 isn't a circle. It's a front line."
The GoFundMe pinged again. $247,200.
Nova didn't hear it. She was already pulling the next thread.
